documents.text: Milvus is an open-source vector database built for scalable similarity search and AI applications. It supports billions of vectors with millisecond search latency.
Vector embeddings are numerical representations of data that capture semantic meaning. They enable machines to understand and compare content based on similarity.
Semantic search goes beyond keyword matching by understanding the intent and contextual meaning of search queries. It uses vector embeddings to find relevant results.
Hybrid search combines the strengths of keyword-based search (BM25) and semantic search (vector similarity) to provide more accurate and comprehensive results.
Machine learning models like BERT, GPT, and sentence transformers can generate high-quality embeddings from text, images, and other data types.
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) combines vector databases with large language models to provide accurate, context-aware responses grounded in your data.
Index types in Milvus include IVF_FLAT for balanced performance, HNSW for speed, and IVF_SQ8 for memory efficiency. Choose based on your use case.
Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors, making it ideal for comparing embeddings. Values range from -1 to 1, with 1 indicating identical direction.
Recommendation systems use vector similarity to suggest products, content, or services based on user preferences and behavior patterns.
Image search applications convert images to vectors using CNN models, enabling visual similarity search across large image databases.
Question answering systems leverage vector databases to retrieve relevant context from knowledge bases before generating answers.
Batch processing in Milvus allows efficient insertion of thousands of vectors at once, significantly improving throughput for large-scale data ingestion.
Metadata filtering enables precise queries by combining vector similarity with structured data filters like date ranges, categories, or tags.
Partitioning in Milvus divides collections into smaller segments, improving query performance and enabling efficient data management at scale.
Anomaly detection systems use vector embeddings to identify unusual patterns or outliers in data by measuring distance from normal behavior clusters.
Multi-modal search combines text, image, and audio embeddings in a unified vector space, enabling cross-modal retrieval and understanding.
Fine-tuning embedding models on domain-specific data improves retrieval accuracy for specialized applications like medical or legal search.
Vector quantization techniques like product quantization reduce memory footprint while maintaining acceptable search accuracy for large-scale deployments.
Real-time search applications require low-latency vector databases that can handle concurrent queries while maintaining high throughput.
Duplicate detection uses vector similarity to identify near-duplicate content in large document collections, helping maintain data quality.
